steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has a compound predicate: "This technique emphasizes reacting naturally to one's environment in a scene and ______ actors to focus less on their own movement and speech." Both verbs share the singular subject "This technique" and must be in the same form. The first verb is "emphasizes" (third-person singular, simple present).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'encourages': Third-person singular, simple present—the same form as "emphasizes." A singular subject with two parallel simple-present verbs is grammatically correct. ✓
  • Choice B — 'have encouraged': Present perfect plural. "This technique" is singular, so "have" is wrong (singular would require "has"). Also shifts tense from simple present to present perfect without reason. ✗
  • Choice C — 'are encouraging': Present progressive plural. "This technique" is singular, so "are" is wrong. It also breaks the parallel with "emphasizes" by switching to a progressive form. ✗
  • Choice D — 'encourage': Base form (or plural present). With a singular subject "This technique," the verb must be third-person singular ("encourages"), not the base form "encourage." ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
